Authors: Xiao Hu; Wenbin Zhu; Haizhen Wei; Runsheng Yin; Yongxiang Li; Yiwei Rong; Di Chen

Journal: Chemical Geology

DOI: 10.1016/j.chemgeo.2026.123558

Relevance: 0.588 (consider)

Tonalite-trondhjemite-granodiorite (TTG), the main constituent of the Archean Earth's continental crust, is widely believed to originate from the partial melting of hydrated metamorphic basalts. However, the tectonic setting of the TTG's origin and thus the Archean Earth evolution remains poorly understood. In this study, we performed silicon (Si) isotope, mercury (Hg) isotope, and geochemical data analysis on 3.06 Ga to 2.5 Ga TTG samples from the Dunhuang Block, at the eastern margin of the Tarim Craton, to investigate their protolith composition and the formation environment. The δ30Si values of quartz in these Dunhuang TTG samples range from +0.05 ± 0.07‰ (2SD) to +0.25 ± 0.08‰ (2SD). These consistently heavy quartz Si isotope compositions may indicate that the silicified oceanic crust was incorporated into their protoliths. The Δ199Hg values of the 3.06–2.7 Ga Dunhuang TTG samples range from −0.05‰ to +0.09‰. In contrast, the 2.6–2.5 Ga samples exhibit a wider range (−0.11‰ to +0.14‰), indicating that the younger Dunhuang TTG protolith composition were no longer homogeneous and incorporated supracrustal materials. Coupled SiHg isotopes suggest that supracrustal recycling was a key process in the generation of the Dunhuang TTGs, and that the protolith composition of the 2.6–2.5 Ga Dunhuang TTGs experienced a change compared to the earlier Dunhuang TTG samples. Combined with phase equilibrium modeling and geochemical data, we speculate that the subduction model may represent a reasonable explanation for the formation environment of 2.6–2.5 Ga Dunhuang TTGs, although non-subduction models cannot be ruled out.

item.title: Spatial-temporal distribution of late Cenozoic intraplate basalts in the Leiqiong area, South China: Insights into the distinct roles of mantle plume and continental lithosphere during plume head emplacement
item.dc:creator: Guo-Hong Sun
Xiao-Long Huang
Fan Yang
Yang Yu
Sheng-Zhu Zhu
item.description: The emplacement of plume head is a critical aspect of mantle plume theory; however, the respective roles of the plume and lithosphere during the emplacement remain insufficiently understood. The late Cenozoic Hainan mantle plume, inferred to remain in the plume head stage, preserves records of its emplacement processes. This study presents comprehensive geochemical data, including major and trace elements, as well as Sr–Nd–Pb–Hf isotopes, for late Cenozoic basalts from the northern Leiqiong area (including Leizhou Peninsula, northern Hainan Island, and Weizhou Island), which are associated with the Hainan plume. Based on their spatial-temporal distribution, these basalts are categorized into early and late stages. Early-stage basalts, restricted to eastern Leiqiong area, show positive ΔεHf values (0.51–2.34) and consistently low Ce/Pb (7.22–18.24) and Nb/Th (6.78–10.52) ratios, suggesting a significant contribution from recycled sedimentary components. Late-stage basalts, distributed across the northern Leiqiong area, are subdivided into two groups based on their ΔεHf values. The eastern late-stage basalts display positive ΔεHf values with overall low Ce/Pb and Nb/Th ratios, whereas the western late-stage basalts show negative ΔεHf values (−1.71 to −0.01) with overall higher Ce/Pb (14.59–31.78) and Nb/Th (10.64–12.77) ratios, indicating a substantial contribution of recycled oceanic crust in their mantle source. The spatial-temporal distribution and geochemical variations of the Leiqiong basalts highlight distinct roles of the plume and continental lithosphere during plume head emplacement. The lithosphere architecture strongly controls the ascent and emplacement of the plume head because the plume lacks sufficient vigor, owing to the impediment of significant stagnant slabs in the mantle transition zone beneath the Leiqiong area. Thus, the Hainan mantle plume initially ascended through a lithospheric weak zone in eastern Leiqiong area and subsequently propagated laterally across the northern Leiqiong area due to lateral variations in lithospheric architecture. The mantle sources of both stages were influenced by the Hainan plume during its ascent and lateral propagation. As the plume expanded, fusible recycled sedimentary components accumulated beneath the lithospheric weak zone, while the surrounding mantle was depleted due to tectonic suction toward the central region. Consequently, late-stage basalts in northwestern Leiqiong primarily record a signature of recycled oceanic crust introduced by the plume. Therefore, the plume governs the compositional evolution of the mantle sources during its propagation. Published: 2026-06-07T14:25:32+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.587 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Guo-Hong Sun; Xiao-Long Huang; Fan Yang; Yang Yu; Sheng-Zhu Zhu

Journal: Lithos

DOI: 10.1016/j.lithos.2026.108664

Relevance: 0.587 (consider)

The emplacement of plume head is a critical aspect of mantle plume theory; however, the respective roles of the plume and lithosphere during the emplacement remain insufficiently understood. The late Cenozoic Hainan mantle plume, inferred to remain in the plume head stage, preserves records of its emplacement processes. This study presents comprehensive geochemical data, including major and trace elements, as well as Sr–Nd–Pb–Hf isotopes, for late Cenozoic basalts from the northern Leiqiong area (including Leizhou Peninsula, northern Hainan Island, and Weizhou Island), which are associated with the Hainan plume. Based on their spatial-temporal distribution, these basalts are categorized into early and late stages. Early-stage basalts, restricted to eastern Leiqiong area, show positive ΔεHf values (0.51–2.34) and consistently low Ce/Pb (7.22–18.24) and Nb/Th (6.78–10.52) ratios, suggesting a significant contribution from recycled sedimentary components. Late-stage basalts, distributed across the northern Leiqiong area, are subdivided into two groups based on their ΔεHf values. The eastern late-stage basalts display positive ΔεHf values with overall low Ce/Pb and Nb/Th ratios, whereas the western late-stage basalts show negative ΔεHf values (−1.71 to −0.01) with overall higher Ce/Pb (14.59–31.78) and Nb/Th (10.64–12.77) ratios, indicating a substantial contribution of recycled oceanic crust in their mantle source. The spatial-temporal distribution and geochemical variations of the Leiqiong basalts highlight distinct roles of the plume and continental lithosphere during plume head emplacement. The lithosphere architecture strongly controls the ascent and emplacement of the plume head because the plume lacks sufficient vigor, owing to the impediment of significant stagnant slabs in the mantle transition zone beneath the Leiqiong area. Thus, the Hainan mantle plume initially ascended through a lithospheric weak zone in eastern Leiqiong area and subsequently propagated laterally across the northern Leiqiong area due to lateral variations in lithospheric architecture. The mantle sources of both stages were influenced by the Hainan plume during its ascent and lateral propagation. As the plume expanded, fusible recycled sedimentary components accumulated beneath the lithospheric weak zone, while the surrounding mantle was depleted due to tectonic suction toward the central region. Consequently, late-stage basalts in northwestern Leiqiong primarily record a signature of recycled oceanic crust introduced by the plume. Therefore, the plume governs the compositional evolution of the mantle sources during its propagation.

item.title: Crustal evolution of the Archean Rae Province (northern Canada) from oxygen and hafnium isotopes in zircon
item.dc:creator: Jamie A. Cutts
William J. Davis
Quinn Emon
Richard A. Stern
item.description: The isotope characteristics of the continental crust are fundamental to understanding its formation and evolution. We present zircon O and Hf isotope data for 115 Archean magmatic rocks (>2.58 Ga) from the Rae Province (northern Canada), providing constraints that have been missing from the global Archean archive thus far. Zircon from >2.65 Ga, dominantly tonalite−trondhjemite−granodiorite samples, show mantle-like δ18O, while εHfinitial and two-stage depleted mantle model ages (TDM) are variable and define four broad isotope domains. Two domains comprise relatively juvenile crust (TDM < 3.0 Ga) that define a central spine of the Rae Province. These are flanked to the west and east by two separate domains of more evolved crust with Meso- to Eoarchean histories (TDM > 3.0 Ga). A decrease in εHfinitial with age within each isotope domain is most consistent with infracrustal reworking of local crust. Relative to pre−2.65 Ga crust, zircon from 2.65−2.58 Ga potassic granitoids show a greater range in δ18O, whereas their εHfinitial are more homogenized. Zircon yielding δ18O >6.5‰ are dominant in the northern Rae Province, where sedimentary sequences are most abundant and likely reflect widespread erosion and sediment/crustal recycling following the emergence of >2.65 Ga crust. The relatively homogeneous εHfinitial is most consistent with an infracrustal origin and mixing with local sediment. The zircon isotope data are used to evaluate and refine boundaries of crustal blocks, and to interrogate models for the construction of the Rae Province. The isotope signatures of the Rae Province are consistent with the global Archean archive for the emergence of the depleted mantle and cratonization. Published: 2026-06-09T20:04:51+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.581 (consider)

Authors: Jamie A. Cutts; William J. Davis; Quinn Emon; Richard A. Stern

Journal: Geological Society of America Bulletin

The isotope characteristics of the continental crust are fundamental to understanding its formation and evolution. We present zircon O and Hf isotope data for 115 Archean magmatic rocks (>2.58 Ga) from the Rae Province (northern Canada), providing constraints that have been missing from the global Archean archive thus far. Zircon from >2.65 Ga, dominantly tonalite−trondhjemite−granodiorite samples, show mantle-like δ18O, while εHfinitial and two-stage depleted mantle model ages (TDM) are variable and define four broad isotope domains. Two domains comprise relatively juvenile crust (TDM < 3.0 Ga) that define a central spine of the Rae Province. These are flanked to the west and east by two separate domains of more evolved crust with Meso- to Eoarchean histories (TDM > 3.0 Ga). A decrease in εHfinitial with age within each isotope domain is most consistent with infracrustal reworking of local crust. Relative to pre−2.65 Ga crust, zircon from 2.65−2.58 Ga potassic granitoids show a greater range in δ18O, whereas their εHfinitial are more homogenized. Zircon yielding δ18O >6.5‰ are dominant in the northern Rae Province, where sedimentary sequences are most abundant and likely reflect widespread erosion and sediment/crustal recycling following the emergence of >2.65 Ga crust. The relatively homogeneous εHfinitial is most consistent with an infracrustal origin and mixing with local sediment. The zircon isotope data are used to evaluate and refine boundaries of crustal blocks, and to interrogate models for the construction of the Rae Province. The isotope signatures of the Rae Province are consistent with the global Archean archive for the emergence of the depleted mantle and cratonization.

item.title: Ti-poor high-Al and high-Cr podiform chromitites in the Ngape ophiolite, myanmar: constraints from mineral chemistry and Re-Os isotopes
item.dc:creator: Toe Toe Kyaw Lin
Hong-Fu Zhang
A-Bing Lin
Juan Zhang
item.description: The Ngape ophiolite in western Myanmar preserves critical records of Neo-Tethyan Ocean tectonic evolution. Here, we present the integrated mineral chemistry, whole-rock platinum group element (PGE), and Re-Os isotopic study of podiform chromitites from this ophiolite to constrain their petrogenesis and tectonic setting. The chromitites are classified into high-Al (Cr# = 56.06–60.06) and high-Cr (Cr# = 63.01–72.63) types. Both types are notably poor in TiO2 (<0.29 wt%). Calculated parental melts indicate that the high-Al chromitites crystallized from the tholeiitic melts, whereas the high-Cr chromitites originated from boninitic melts. The chromitites exhibit enrichment of IPGE (Os, Ir, Ru) over PPGE (Pt, Pd, Rh), and possess unradiogenic, homogeneous 187Os/188Os ratios (0.12638–0.12863; average 0.12695). These PGE and Os isotopic signatures point to a depleted mantle source. Trace elements (e.g., V, Sc, Ga) further reveal that the high-Cr chromitites formed under higher oxygen fugacity and from a more depleted source than the high-Al variety. The coexistence of these Ti-poor chromitites records a progressive magmatic evolution within a forearc mantle wedge, transitioning from nascent subduction initiation (high-Al type) to a geochemically mature stage of the subduction initiation sequence (high-Cr type). This study provides the comprehensive Re-Os isotopic dataset for Myanmar ophiolites and documents a complete, in-situ magmatic progression during forearc evolution in the Neo-Tethyan realm. Published: 2026-06-07T14:26:51+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.581 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Toe Toe Kyaw Lin; Hong-Fu Zhang; A-Bing Lin; Juan Zhang

Journal: Ore Geology Reviews

DOI: 10.1016/j.oregeorev.2026.107358

Relevance: 0.581 (consider)

The Ngape ophiolite in western Myanmar preserves critical records of Neo-Tethyan Ocean tectonic evolution. Here, we present the integrated mineral chemistry, whole-rock platinum group element (PGE), and Re-Os isotopic study of podiform chromitites from this ophiolite to constrain their petrogenesis and tectonic setting. The chromitites are classified into high-Al (Cr# = 56.06–60.06) and high-Cr (Cr# = 63.01–72.63) types. Both types are notably poor in TiO2 (<0.29 wt%). Calculated parental melts indicate that the high-Al chromitites crystallized from the tholeiitic melts, whereas the high-Cr chromitites originated from boninitic melts. The chromitites exhibit enrichment of IPGE (Os, Ir, Ru) over PPGE (Pt, Pd, Rh), and possess unradiogenic, homogeneous 187Os/188Os ratios (0.12638–0.12863; average 0.12695). These PGE and Os isotopic signatures point to a depleted mantle source. Trace elements (e.g., V, Sc, Ga) further reveal that the high-Cr chromitites formed under higher oxygen fugacity and from a more depleted source than the high-Al variety. The coexistence of these Ti-poor chromitites records a progressive magmatic evolution within a forearc mantle wedge, transitioning from nascent subduction initiation (high-Al type) to a geochemically mature stage of the subduction initiation sequence (high-Cr type). This study provides the comprehensive Re-Os isotopic dataset for Myanmar ophiolites and documents a complete, in-situ magmatic progression during forearc evolution in the Neo-Tethyan realm.

item.title: Transformation of Refractory Oceanic Lithospheric Mantle by Reactive Melt Infiltration: An Experimental Study on the Roles of Temperature, Melt Volume and Ascent Velocity
item.dc:creator: Yong‐Sheng Hou
Hong‐Yan Li
Chao Zhang
Yu Wang
Yi‐Gang Xu
item.prism:publicationName: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth
item.description: Abstract Reactive melt infiltration critically modifies the physical and chemical properties of the oceanic lithospheric mantle (OLM). This process, involving melt‐rock reactions and in situ crystallization, exhibits substantial spatial and temporal variability driven by melt volume and ascent velocity. To explore how eclogite/pyroxenite‐derived plume melts transform refractory OLM, we conducted coupled reaction and in situ crystallization experiments at 2 GPa, systematically varying temperature, melt/rock ratio, and duration. We demonstrate that higher temperatures, lower melt/rock ratios, and shorter durations promote only orthopyroxene precipitation coupled with olivine dissolution, thereby preserving the starting harzburgitic lithology. In contrast, lower temperatures, higher melt/rock ratios, and longer durations facilitate clinopyroxene and orthopyroxene crystallization, converting refractory OLM harzburgite into fertile lherzolite or localized websterite. Beyond the well‐established influences of pressure and temperature, our study underscores the pivotal roles of melt volume and ascent velocity in governing metasomatism. This study reveals that clinopyroxene formed by high‐pressure (2 GPa) reactive melt infiltration explains clinopyroxene compositions in ocean island basalt–hosted mantle xenoliths, including high Na 2 O and TiO 2 and low Mg# and Cr#. Furthermore, these mineralogical transformations significantly alter OLM geophysical properties, reducing shear‐wave velocity by ∼2.2%–5.4% and increasing density by ∼1.4%–2.2%. We therefore propose that reactive melt infiltration explains shallow (50–60 km depth) low‐velocity anomalies observed in mature oceanic lithosphere (90–110 km thick) above a mantle plume. Concurrently, the associated density increase may induce delamination of metasomatized OLM, accompanied by buoyant ascent of underlying high‐temperature melting residues, ultimately driving vertical mantle convection within the plume. Published: 2026-06-05T06:25:25+00:00 Venue: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth Score: 0.579 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yong‐Sheng Hou; Hong‐Yan Li; Chao Zhang; Yu Wang; Yi‐Gang Xu

Journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth

DOI: 10.1029/2026jb033875

Relevance: 0.579 (consider)

Abstract Reactive melt infiltration critically modifies the physical and chemical properties of the oceanic lithospheric mantle (OLM). This process, involving melt‐rock reactions and in situ crystallization, exhibits substantial spatial and temporal variability driven by melt volume and ascent velocity. To explore how eclogite/pyroxenite‐derived plume melts transform refractory OLM, we conducted coupled reaction and in situ crystallization experiments at 2 GPa, systematically varying temperature, melt/rock ratio, and duration. We demonstrate that higher temperatures, lower melt/rock ratios, and shorter durations promote only orthopyroxene precipitation coupled with olivine dissolution, thereby preserving the starting harzburgitic lithology. In contrast, lower temperatures, higher melt/rock ratios, and longer durations facilitate clinopyroxene and orthopyroxene crystallization, converting refractory OLM harzburgite into fertile lherzolite or localized websterite. Beyond the well‐established influences of pressure and temperature, our study underscores the pivotal roles of melt volume and ascent velocity in governing metasomatism. This study reveals that clinopyroxene formed by high‐pressure (2 GPa) reactive melt infiltration explains clinopyroxene compositions in ocean island basalt–hosted mantle xenoliths, including high Na 2 O and TiO 2 and low Mg# and Cr#. Furthermore, these mineralogical transformations significantly alter OLM geophysical properties, reducing shear‐wave velocity by ∼2.2%–5.4% and increasing density by ∼1.4%–2.2%. We therefore propose that reactive melt infiltration explains shallow (50–60 km depth) low‐velocity anomalies observed in mature oceanic lithosphere (90–110 km thick) above a mantle plume. Concurrently, the associated density increase may induce delamination of metasomatized OLM, accompanied by buoyant ascent of underlying high‐temperature melting residues, ultimately driving vertical mantle convection within the plume.

item.title: Magmatic evolution and rotation dynamics of Gorontalo Eastern Mountain complex, North Sulawesi Arc, Indonesia, and their tectonic implications
item.dc:creator: Intan Noviantari Manyoe
Gayatri Indah Marliyani
Agung Harijoko
Wahyudi
Michelle Nadya Putri
Anang Sahroni
Mochamad Nukman
Florian Lhuillier
item.description: Previous studies have attributed magmatism in the Gorontalo Eastern Mountain Complex (GEMC) to subduction-related processes, slab delamination, or extensional tectonics. However, the timing of magmatic activity and its relationship to changes in subduction geometry and vertical-axis rotation remain poorly constrained. This study presents a new integrated dataset that combines 40K-40Ar geochronology, major- and trace-element geochemistry, and paleomagnetic data to constrain the magmatic and tectonic significance of the GEMC. New and published data indicate that tholeiitic to calc-alkaline volcanic rocks, ranging from basalt to rhyolite, were emplaced between ~44.6 Ma and ~ 0.16 Ma. The Tinombo basement formed during Eocene back-arc spreading in the Celebes Sea, likely driven by rollback of southward-subducting plates. The Lower Member of the Bilungala Volcanics was emplaced during the Oligocene to Miocene in association with northward subduction, followed by a transition to subduction related to the Sangihe. Subsequent magmatism, represented by the Middle Member of the Bilungala Volcanics and the Motomboto–Pinogu Volcanics, occurred from the Pliocene to the Pleistocene and is linked to the North Sulawesi subduction. Paleomagnetic results reveal significant clockwise vertical-axis rotation in the Gorontalo (39.6° ± 7.7°), established by the Middle Miocene and likely facilitated by the geometry of surrounding slabs and accommodated by right-lateral strike-slip faulting along the arc. The results indicate that progressive reorganization of subduction geometry exerted control on arc magmatism and block rotation. This study provides new constraints on the tectono-magmatic evolution of the North Sulawesi Arc and offers a conceptual framework for understanding the dynamics of a structurally and magmatically segmented island-arc system at a convergent plate boundary. Published: 2026-06-10T23:56:56+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.572 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Intan Noviantari Manyoe; Gayatri Indah Marliyani; Agung Harijoko; Wahyudi; Michelle Nadya Putri; Anang Sahroni; Mochamad Nukman; Florian Lhuillier

Journal: Lithos

DOI: 10.1016/j.lithos.2026.108671

Relevance: 0.572 (consider)

Previous studies have attributed magmatism in the Gorontalo Eastern Mountain Complex (GEMC) to subduction-related processes, slab delamination, or extensional tectonics. However, the timing of magmatic activity and its relationship to changes in subduction geometry and vertical-axis rotation remain poorly constrained. This study presents a new integrated dataset that combines 40K-40Ar geochronology, major- and trace-element geochemistry, and paleomagnetic data to constrain the magmatic and tectonic significance of the GEMC. New and published data indicate that tholeiitic to calc-alkaline volcanic rocks, ranging from basalt to rhyolite, were emplaced between ~44.6 Ma and ~ 0.16 Ma. The Tinombo basement formed during Eocene back-arc spreading in the Celebes Sea, likely driven by rollback of southward-subducting plates. The Lower Member of the Bilungala Volcanics was emplaced during the Oligocene to Miocene in association with northward subduction, followed by a transition to subduction related to the Sangihe. Subsequent magmatism, represented by the Middle Member of the Bilungala Volcanics and the Motomboto–Pinogu Volcanics, occurred from the Pliocene to the Pleistocene and is linked to the North Sulawesi subduction. Paleomagnetic results reveal significant clockwise vertical-axis rotation in the Gorontalo (39.6° ± 7.7°), established by the Middle Miocene and likely facilitated by the geometry of surrounding slabs and accommodated by right-lateral strike-slip faulting along the arc. The results indicate that progressive reorganization of subduction geometry exerted control on arc magmatism and block rotation. This study provides new constraints on the tectono-magmatic evolution of the North Sulawesi Arc and offers a conceptual framework for understanding the dynamics of a structurally and magmatically segmented island-arc system at a convergent plate boundary.

item.title: Crust–Mantle Architecture Remodelled by Mantle Plumes: Evidence From Late Permian (ca. 260 Ma) Gabbro–Monzonite Association in the Inner Zone of the Emeishan Large Igneous Province
item.dc:creator: Yihang Ma
Jiangfeng Qin
Shaocong Lai
Xiaoping Long
Yunpeng Yang
Renzhi Zhu
item.description: ABSTRACT Crust–mantle architecture in large igneous provinces (LIPs) controls magma generation and crustal growth. The coeval gabbro and monzonite in the inner zone of the Emeishan Large Igneous Province (ELIP) provide a key opportunity. They display different whole‐rock and mineral compositions, indicating different sources and melting conditions. The gabbro displays moderate TiO 2 and MgO contents, with Ti/Y ratios of 408–413. Its elevated Nb/Ta and Zr/Hf ratios, evolved zircon Hf isotope, and apatite chemistry suggest a volatile‐poor primitive melt, which was derived from the melting of sub‐continental lithospheric mantle. The monzonite displays high Zr and Nb contents, TFeO/MgO and 10,000 × Ga/Al ratios, indicating high‐temperature melting of mafic lower crust, the high Sr contents suggest plagioclase incorporation into the melts. Its relatively depleted isotopic compositions ( ε Nd ( t ) = +3.2 to +3.7; ε Hf ( t ) = +0.87 to +0.88) reveal a juvenile crust source region. Apatite in the monzonite indicates a volatile‐rich melt, supporting a high‐temperature crustal melting origin. This geochemical dichotomy illuminates a two‐stage crustal evolution model: (1) melting of plume‐modified lithospheric mantle to generate the gabbro, followed by (2) thermal remelting of the juvenile mafic crust to form the monzonite. Such plume‐induced crust–mantle interaction offers a window into understanding crust–mantle architecture in global LIPs. Published: 2026-06-09T12:41:40+00:00 Venue: Acta Geologica Sinica - English Edition Score: 0.571 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yihang Ma; Jiangfeng Qin; Shaocong Lai; Xiaoping Long; Yunpeng Yang; Renzhi Zhu

Journal: Acta Geologica Sinica - English Edition

DOI: 10.1111/1755-6724.70079

Relevance: 0.571 (consider)

ABSTRACT Crust–mantle architecture in large igneous provinces (LIPs) controls magma generation and crustal growth. The coeval gabbro and monzonite in the inner zone of the Emeishan Large Igneous Province (ELIP) provide a key opportunity. They display different whole‐rock and mineral compositions, indicating different sources and melting conditions. The gabbro displays moderate TiO 2 and MgO contents, with Ti/Y ratios of 408–413. Its elevated Nb/Ta and Zr/Hf ratios, evolved zircon Hf isotope, and apatite chemistry suggest a volatile‐poor primitive melt, which was derived from the melting of sub‐continental lithospheric mantle. The monzonite displays high Zr and Nb contents, TFeO/MgO and 10,000 × Ga/Al ratios, indicating high‐temperature melting of mafic lower crust, the high Sr contents suggest plagioclase incorporation into the melts. Its relatively depleted isotopic compositions ( ε Nd ( t ) = +3.2 to +3.7; ε Hf ( t ) = +0.87 to +0.88) reveal a juvenile crust source region. Apatite in the monzonite indicates a volatile‐rich melt, supporting a high‐temperature crustal melting origin. This geochemical dichotomy illuminates a two‐stage crustal evolution model: (1) melting of plume‐modified lithospheric mantle to generate the gabbro, followed by (2) thermal remelting of the juvenile mafic crust to form the monzonite. Such plume‐induced crust–mantle interaction offers a window into understanding crust–mantle architecture in global LIPs.
